critic_raw:
This is a well-constructed experimental design with genuine attention to leakage, temporal structure, and confound control. I'll work through the real issues I can identify.

**Issue 1: The comparison conflates feature set and model class simultaneously**

The core claim is that 'event-level granularity and temporal structure' drives the improvement. The design acknowledges this explicitly. But the treatment arm uses XGBoost/LightGBM and the control arm uses logistic regression. Even if the same aggregate features are fed to both, the capacity difference is substantial. The missing cell is: XGBoost trained on aggregate features only. Without this cell, any observed performance gap cannot be attributed to feature granularity vs. model capacity. A logistic regression on event-level features would complete the 2x2. The design claims to isolate 'the value of event-level granularity,' but the ablation is incomplete — it's a confounded 2-arm comparison, not a factorial design.

Evidence one way or the other: Run XGBoost on the aggregate feature set. If its AUPRC matches the event-level XGBoost, the feature set matters. If it matches logistic regression on aggregate features, the model class matters. If XGBoost on aggregates outperforms logistic regression on aggregates substantially, the claimed isolation fails.

**Issue 2: Stratified churn rate across splits undermines the temporal validity the design is trying to achieve**

Section 2 states the chronological split is mandatory because 'the model must generalize forward in time, not interpolate.' Then Section 2 also states that 'churn rates are stratified to ensure class distribution is balanced across splits.' These goals are in tension. Real churn rates change over time — seasonally, due to content cycles, pricing changes, competitive pressure. If churn was 3% in months 1-12 and 5% in months 16-18, stratifying to equalize these distributions modifies the test set away from the actual future distribution the model will face in production. The calibration metrics (ECE, Brier score) will be evaluated against a stratified distribution that doesn't match deployment, and any threshold selected at 80% precision on a balanced test set will be miscalibrated in production. This directly contradicts the paper's stated goal of reflecting 'the true deployment scenario.'

Evidence one way or the other: Report the raw churn rates in each split before and after stratification. If they differ by more than a few percentage points, the stratification is materially distorting the evaluation. Compare calibration metrics on the raw vs. stratified test set. The Brier score and ECE will diverge if stratification is significant.

**Issue 3: The 7-day observation window creates a silent right-censoring problem for the label**

The label is defined as cancellation within 30 days after the observation window. The 2-week finalization lag is applied to cancellation records. But consider a user whose observation window ends on day X: the label is finalized at X+30+14 = X+44 days. For users near the end of the test period (Month 18), labels may not be fully finalized at the time the experiment is evaluated, creating a systematic bias where recent users are more likely to be labeled as non-churned (not yet cancelled) than users from earlier in the dataset. This is survivorship/censoring bias asymmetrically affecting the test set's most recent portion. The model trained on fully-labeled historical data will be evaluated on a test set where the most recent labels are systematically noisier.

Evidence one way or the other: Plot label finalization completeness as a function of the user's observation window end date. If the fraction of confirmed cancellations drops in the final 6 weeks of the test period, the issue is real. Compare AUPRC on the early vs. late half of the test set; degradation at the tail is a signature of this censoring effect.

**Issue 4: Downgrade exclusion may systematically remove users who are highest-risk signals**

Users who downgrade rather than cancel outright are excluded. In subscription businesses, downgrade-then-cancel is a common behavioral trajectory — downgraders are often the 'soft churn' population with the most distinctive behavioral fingerprints. Excluding them removes a potentially informative class from training, which means the model is never exposed to patterns that precede soft churn, and may be evaluated on a population that is artificially easy to classify (only hard cancellations vs. definitely retained). In production, the model will encounter downgraders and silently assign them non-churn scores, producing a predictable failure mode the experiment cannot detect. This is not just a scope limitation — it affects whether the learned signal transfers to deployment.

Evidence one way or the other: Report the downgrader rate as a fraction of all subscription-ending events. If it exceeds ~15%, the excluded population is large enough to materially affect deployment performance. Run the trained model on downgraders post-hoc (without including them in evaluation) and examine their score distribution — if they cluster near 0.5, the model has no signal for them.

**Summary:**

Issue 1 (factorial design incompleteness) is the most fundamental — it directly undermines the stated causal claim about what drives performance. Issue 2 (stratification contradicting temporal validity) is a real flaw that will distort calibration metrics specifically. Issues 3 and 4 are genuine but bounded — they create known-direction biases rather than rendering the experiment invalid. Issues 3 and 4 alone would not be disqualifying, but Issues 1 and 2 together are methodological flaws that affect the core claims.
all_issues_raised: The comparison conflates feature granularity and model class simultaneously — the missing ablation cell (XGBoost on aggregate features) means the performance gap cannot be attributed to event-level features vs. model capacity.

Stratifying churn rates to be balanced across chronological splits distorts the test set away from the true future deployment distribution, undermining both the stated temporal validity goal and the calibration metrics.

The 2-week finalization lag combined with a 30-day label window creates systematic right-censoring bias for users near the end of the test period, asymmetrically degrading label quality for the most recent test observations.

Excluding downgraders removes a soft-churn population with potentially distinctive behavioral signals, creating a silent failure mode in production where the model has no learned representation for the downgrade-then-cancel trajectory.
all_issues_adjudicated: The comparison conflates feature granularity and model class simultaneously — the missing ablation cell (XGBoost on aggregate features) means the performance gap cannot be attributed to event-level features vs. model capacity.

Stratifying churn rates to be balanced across chronological splits distorts the test set away from the true future deployment distribution, undermining both the stated temporal validity goal and the calibration metrics.

The 2-week finalization lag combined with a 30-day label window creates systematic right-censoring bias for users near the end of the test period, asymmetrically degrading label quality for the most recent test observations.

Excluding downgraders removes a soft-churn population with potentially distinctive behavioral signals, creating a silent failure mode in production where the model has no learned representation for the downgrade-then-cancel trajectory.
